retrieved_docs.title: Ancylostoma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical symptoms of Ancylostoma infection can be misleading because the symptoms are also present with other infections and nutritional-deficiency status. Routine blood findings can reveal iron-deficient anemia, peripheral blood eosinophilia, and sometimes, elevated IgE levels. Chest radiography may reveal diffuse alveolar infiltrates during the migration phase, but this is not helpful when the worms have already invaded the gut. These tests should be interpreted carefully since other helminth infections such as strongyloidiasis and ascariasis show the same blood and radiologic profile.

retrieved_docs.title: Strongyloidiasis (Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Among individuals who have asymptomatic chronic infection, incidental diagnosis of peripheral eosinophilia is the most common presentation. However, at the acute stage, infected individuals may have a pruritic edematous petechial rash at the site of penetration. A more pathognomonic rash is larva currens or migrans from the migration of the filariform larva under the skin resulting in a hypersensitivity response in the host. It results in a transient linear erythematous serpiginous rash that shifts at a rate of 2-10 cm/hour. The duration of the rash can be hours to days at a time, but it can be a recurrent process during the duration of parasitic autoinfection. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Strongyloidiasis -- History and Physical -- Acute Infection
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infection may show an itchy serpiginous skin rash in the area where the larvae penetrate the skin. When filariform larvae in fecal-contaminated soil enter the body through skin penetration, intense pruritus, congestion, and edema is noted at the portal of entry. [3] This is known as ground itch and is usually present in feet or hands but can be perianal, abdominal, or virtually anywhere in the body. This intense urticaria can last for up to 3 weeks. [16] Intradermal migration is very fast (5 cm to 15 cm an hour), and the rash it causes is known as larva currens. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Trichinosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Trichinosis presents with eosinophilia and intestinal symptoms in early infection. Therefore, several parasitic infections may cause the patients’ symptoms. These include: strongyloidiasis schistosomiasis hookworm gnathostomiasis lymphatic filariasis ascariasis whipworm

retrieved_docs.title: Helminthiasis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Other phyla platyhelminths also called flatworms, which include flukes schistosomes and tapeworms such as the pork tapeworm that causes cysticercosis. Flukes are known as trematodes, and tapeworms are known as cestodes. [1] [6] Soil-transmitted helminthiasis is a roundworm ( Ascaris lumbricoides ), whipworm ( Trichuris trichiura ), and hookworm ( Ancylostoma duodenale and Necator americanus ). [4] [3] [5]

retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the nematode Ascaris lumbricoides in the etiology of ascariasis. Describe the pathophysiology of ascariasis. Summarize the use of a stool exam to detect ova and parasites in the evaluation of ascariasis. Outline the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team members to enhance the delivery of care for patients affected with ascariasis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Ascariasis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A female worm can produce up to 200,000 eggs a day. This makes retrieval from a stool exam easier. The best diagnostic test is still the stool exam for ova and parasites, searching for large oval brown trilayered eggs with a mamillated coat. It is important to note that stool can be negative while the worm migrates and matures (approximately one month). Only when worms are mature do they start secreting eggs. Sometimes an adult worm can be seen in the stool or coming out of the rectum but can also be coughed up or passed in the urine. A complete blood count can show eosinophilia during the active migration phase from the intestine to the lungs, and larvae can be found in the sputum. Abdominal x-rays can be sensitive but not specific when a whirlpool sign is present. Ultrasound and CT scans can be used to identify worms in the biliary duct and gallbladder. Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) can be used for diagnosis and treatment. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Strongyloidiasis -- Pathophysiology -- Life Cycle of Strongyloides stercoralis
retrieved_docs.content: The distinctive nature of the life cycle of this human parasite is attributed to its ability to cause autoinfection. Its life cycle comprises a free-living phase and a parasitic phase. [12] The free-living phase begins when rhabditiform larvae passed in stools grow into infective skin-penetrating filariform larvae or develop into adult worms which reproduce sexually to give rise to the second generation of filariform larvae. [12] The parasitic phase of the life cycle begins with skin penetration by the filariform larvae. These larvae then migrate via the bloodstream or lymphatics to reside in the gut. Within the small intestine, these larvae mature into adult females, which reproduce by parthenogenesis and give rise to rhabditiform larvae which are either excreted in feces or develop into filariform larvae to cause autoinfection. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Strongyloidiasis (Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic infection can have nonspecific multiorgan symptoms predominantly of gastrointestinal, dermatological, or respiratory systems. Abdominal bloating, heartburn, constipation or diarrhea, and reduced appetite are the usual gastrointestinal symptoms. They take about 2 weeks to manifest after the initial infection. A chronic dry cough or wheezing that mimics asthma can be the respiratory manifestation, whereas constitutional symptoms of low-grade fever, weight loss, and anemia can also be the sole presenting feature. Larva migrating through pulmonary capillaries can produce alveolar hemorrhage, while their entrance into alveolar spaces causes an eosinophilic inflammatory response resulting in clinical evidence of pneumonitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Strongyloidiasis -- History and Physical -- Acute Infection
retrieved_docs.content: Larva currens or “running” larva is a pathognomonic dermatologic manifestation of strongyloidiasis and can be seen with autoinfection as well. Larva currens manifest as a raised, pink, pruritic, serpiginous streak and can progress approximately 5 to 15 cm per hour. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Strongyloidiasis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the life cycle and mechanism of infection of Strongyloides stercoralis. Describe the clinical presentation of a patient infected with Strongyloides stercoralis. Summarize the medical management of a patient infected with Strongyloides stercoralis. Outline the importance of the interprofessional team in advancing care for patients infected by Strongyloides stercoralis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.content: Acute cholangitis Acute pancreatitis Appendicitis Ascending cholangitis Asthma Cholecystitis and biliary colic Hookworm Intussusception Large bowel obstruction Small bowel obstruction

retrieved_docs.title: Strongyloidiasis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Strongyloidiasis is the term used to indicate infection by Strongyloides stercoralis . It differs from other helminth infections by its ability to cause overwhelming hyperinfection in immunocompromised individuals. [4] Strongyloides infection can last the host's lifetime due to its autoinfection life cycle and cause a wide spectrum of diseases ranging from asymptomatic eosinophilia to severe, life-threatening disseminated disease in immunocompromised patients. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Ancylostoma -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Iron deficiency anemia Intellectual disability Malabsorption Stunted growth Acute gastrointestinal hemorrhage Congestive heart failure

retrieved_docs.title: Helminthiasis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: S. stercoralis infection is commonly asymptomatic, although it may present with eosinophilic pneumonia just like hookworms and A. lumbricoides . Chronic strongyloidiasis might present with anorexia, nausea, weakness, abdominal pain, tenderness, and diarrhea. Larva currens is present as serpiginous urticaria due to chronic infection, mostly over the abdomen, torso, groin, and buttocks. Rarely, reactive arthritis may occur by the immune-mediated mechanisms. Strongyloides hyper-infection syndrome occurs by autoinfection in immunosuppressed individuals who present with intestinal failure or pulmonary failure. If untreated, the mortality ratio is approximately 100%. When large numbers of parasites spread into the different organs of the body, it leads to disseminated strongyloidiasis that present with catastrophic clinical manifestations such as meningitis, disseminated intravascular coagulation, shock, and renal failure. Those who are taking immunosuppressive drugs such as corticosteroid and vincristine, hematological malignancies, hypogammaglobinaemia, and human T-cell lymphotropic virus type 1 infection are vulnerable to develop disseminated strongyloidiasis. [3] [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 346
retrieved_docs.content: Antihelmintika constitute a pharmacologic class of agents used to treat infections caused by helminths (paraziti). This class includes several specific drugs, such as levamisol, albendazol, arprinocid and avermectin, each of which is classified as an antihelmintic substance. Antihelmintika are organized within the broader category “Substance categorized by disposition,” reflecting their classification based on pharmacologic properties. The diseases they target are referred to as Anthelminthica, which are helminth‑related conditions that arise from parasitic infection. While antihelmintika are employed to eradicate the causative parasites of Anthelminthica, the use of these drugs can be associated with adverse drug reactions; such adverse effects are documented under the heading “Adverse effects of medical drugs.” Effective patient care therefore involves selecting appropriate antihelmintic agents from this class, understanding their relationship to the underlying parasitic disease, and monitoring for and managing any drug‑related side effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1455
retrieved_docs.content: Antihelmintika are a class of antiparasitic agents (antiparazitární látky) that are specifically used to combat parasitic organisms (paraziti). Within this therapeutic group, several individual drugs are recognized as subtypes, including levamisol, albendazol, arprinocid, avermectin and paromomycin. Each of these agents inherits the general properties of antihelmintika, providing clinicians with a range of options for treating helminthic infections and other parasitic diseases. Understanding that antihelmintika belong to the broader category of antiparasitic substances and are directly related to the control of parasites helps guide appropriate drug selection and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1132
retrieved_docs.content: Antihelmintika are a subclass of antiparasitic substances and are categorized as substances defined by their disposition. As a broad class of anti‑parasitic agents, antihelmintika encompass several specific drugs that are used to treat helminth infections, including albendazol, arprinocid, avermectin and febantel. Each of these individual agents inherits the general properties of antihelmintika, serving as therapeutic options for eliminating intestinal worms and other parasitic nematodes. Recognizing antihelmintika as part of the larger antiparasitic drug group helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑worm treatments and understand their shared pharmacologic characteristics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 813
retrieved_docs.content: Antihelmintika are classified as substances categorized by disposition, representing a group of agents used to treat helminth infections. Within this broader class, levamisol is identified as a child (subtype) of antihelmintika, indicating that levamisol is a specific antihelmintic medication. Recognizing levamisol as part of the antihelmintika group helps clinicians understand its pharmacological classification and its role among substances categorized by disposition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 907
retrieved_docs.content: Antinematodal agents constitute a therapeutic class of drugs aimed at eliminating nematode parasites, and within this class the filaricida subgroup is recognized as a specific child category that targets filarial worms. These antinematodal agents are also linked to the management of hlístice, a condition caused by helminthic infection, highlighting their broader role in treating various parasitic worm diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1511
retrieved_docs.content: Antihelmintika, also known as anthelmintics, are a class of medications used to treat infections caused by parasites (paraziti). Levamisol is identified as a specific child drug within the antihelmintika group, representing one of the agents that belong to this class. The relationship between antihelmintika and paraziti underscores that these drugs are employed to eliminate or control parasitic organisms, making the connection between antihelmintika, levamisol, and parasites essential for effective management of helminthic diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 533
retrieved_docs.content: Antihelmintika are a class of substances categorized by disposition that are specifically related to parasites, as they are used to treat parasitic infections. The therapeutic class of antihelmintika includes agents such as alatrofloxacin mesylate, which is classified under this anti‑parasitic group. Individual antihelmintic drugs include albendazol, arprinocid, avermectin, and clorsulon, each of which is an example of an antihelmintic agent. By targeting parasites, these medications play a crucial role in managing helminthic diseases and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: Nitroimidazole derivatives, described as antiprotozoal agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, constitute a pharmacologic class that includes several specific compounds. Azanidazole, nimorazol, propenidazole and secnidazole are all members of the nitroimidazole derivative class, sharing the characteristic nitroimidazole chemical scaffold and being employed in the treatment of amoebiasis and various other protozoal infections.
